title: Trigonometry & the Unit Circle
blurb: Radians, the unit circle, sine and cosine as coordinates, and the waves they draw.

lessons.title: Radians
lessons.concreteComparison: Degrees are a Babylonian habit — 360 because their year had ~360 days. Mathematics prefers a unit the circle chooses itself: wrap the RADIUS along the rim, and the angle that arc spans is one **radian**. About $57.3°$, three-and-a-bit of them to a half-turn — and that 'bit' makes the half-turn exactly $\pi$ radians. Nature's angle unit, free of ancient calendars.
lessons.objective: Convert between degrees and radians using $180° = \pi$ rad, and know the key angles in both units.
concept: **One radian** = the angle whose arc equals the radius. The full rim is $2\pi r$, so a full turn holds $2\pi$ radians; a half-turn is $\pi$: the master fact $180° = \pi$ rad.
Convert by proportion: degrees × $\tfrac{\pi}{180}$ = radians; radians × $\tfrac{180}{\pi}$ = degrees. $30° = \tfrac{\pi}{6}$, $45° = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$, $60° = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$, $90° = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$.
Radian answers usually keep the $\pi$: $\tfrac{\pi}{6}$ is exact and readable; $0.5236\ldots$ is neither. Leave $\pi$ in, like leaving $\sqrt2$ unexpanded.
The payoff for 'natural': arc length becomes just $s = r\theta$ (radius × angle) — no $\tfrac{\pi}{180}$ clutter. A 3 m radius swung through 2 rad sweeps exactly 6 m of arc.
lessons.keyIdea: 1 radian = radius-length of arc; 180° = π rad. Convert by ×π/180 or ×180/π; arc length s = rθ.

facts.title: Master fact
facts.explanation: Half-turn = π. Everything converts through it.

workedExamples.statement: A pendulum of length 3 m swings through 2 radians. Arc length?

commonMistakes.text: Memorizing '1 rad = 57.3°' and converting through it.
commonMistakes.correction: Convert through the EXACT master fact $180° = \pi$: $30° = \tfrac{30\pi}{180} = \tfrac{\pi}{6}$. The 57.3 is a decimal approximation — fine for intuition, wrong for exact work.

commonMistakes.text: Dropping the π from radian answers.
commonMistakes.correction: $45°$ is $\tfrac{\pi}{4}$, not $\tfrac14$. The π carries the circle; without it the number means a different (bizarre) angle.

tryIt.statement: Convert $120°$ to radians and $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$ to degrees.

steps.title: Why 360 anyway?
beats: Degrees: 360 per turn — an ancient calendar's leftovers.
Math wants the circle's OWN unit…
…so let the radius measure the rim.

steps.title: One radian at a time
steps.teach: Step the wraps: each radius-length of arc is ONE radian (≈ 57.3°). Watch the third wrap almost reach the π mark at the half-turn — three-and-a-bit radii make it exactly: π ≈ 3.14 radians = 180°. The circle measuring itself.

steps.title: The master fact
steps.explanation: Half of the half-turn: $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$. All conversions route through $180° = \pi$.

steps.title: Route through 180 = π
beats: $\tfrac{3\pi}{4} \to 135°$. Keep the π until the end.

examples.prompt: Fill the key ladder: $30°, 45°, 60°, 90°$ in radians.
steps: Sixths, quarters, thirds, halves — of the half-turn.

examples.prompt: A wheel of radius 40 cm rolls through $\tfrac{3\pi}{2}$ rad. How far does its rim travel?

problems.explanation: The rim is $2\pi$ radii long — so $2\pi$ radians all the way around.

problems.prompt: Radius 6, central angle $\tfrac{\pi}{3}$: the arc is…
problems.explanation: $s = 6 \cdot \tfrac{\pi}{3} = 2\pi$ — one clean multiplication, the radian payoff.

steps.title: The lost constant
steps.body: Some mathematicians campaign for τ (tau) = 2π as the true circle constant — 'one turn = τ' reads cleaner than 'one turn = 2π'. Tau Day (June 28: 6/28) celebrates with two pies. The campaign is only half-joking: radians make angles natural, and τ would make RADIANS natural. π's 300-year head start will probably win anyway.

steps.title: What you learned
points: 1 radian = one radius of arc; $180° = \pi$ rad.
Convert by $\times\tfrac{\pi}{180}$ or $\times\tfrac{180}{\pi}$; keep π exact.
Payoff: $s = r\theta$ — arc length without clutter.

lessons.title: The Unit Circle
lessons.concreteComparison: Geometry's SOH-CAH-TOA lived inside right triangles — useless past $90°$. The upgrade: put a circle of radius 1 at the origin, walk an angle $\theta$ counterclockwise from the positive x-axis, and DEFINE $\cos\theta$ and $\sin\theta$ as the landing point's coordinates. Suddenly every angle — $150°$, $300°$, $1000°$ — has a cosine and sine, read straight off a map.
lessons.objective: Use the unit-circle definition — $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ is the point at angle $\theta$ — including axis angles and quadrant signs.
concept: **The definition**: on a radius-1 circle, the point at angle $\theta$ (counterclockwise from the positive x-axis) IS $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$. Cosine = x-coordinate, sine = y-coordinate.
It agrees with the old triangles: for acute $\theta$, the radius-1 hypotenuse makes adjacent $= \cos\theta$, opposite $= \sin\theta$. Same values — but the circle keeps going past $90°$.

Quadrant signs follow the map: QII has $x < 0, y > 0$ (cos −, sin +); QIII both −; QIV cos +, sin −. And Pythagoras rides along: $\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta = 1$ for every $\theta$ — the point is always on the circle.
lessons.keyIdea: The point at angle θ is (cos θ, sin θ): cosine is x, sine is y. Signs come from the quadrant; cos² + sin² = 1 always.

workedExamples.statement: In which quadrant is $\theta = 200°$, and what are the signs of its cosine and sine?

commonMistakes.text: Assigning sine to x and cosine to y.
commonMistakes.correction: Alphabetical order helps: (x, y) ↔ (Cos, Sin) — C before S. Cosine is the horizontal coordinate, always.

commonMistakes.text: Measuring the angle from the y-axis, or clockwise.
commonMistakes.correction: Convention: start at the positive x-axis, rotate COUNTERclockwise. $90°$ lands at the TOP $(0,1)$ — if yours landed right or bottom, the starting ray or direction slipped.

steps.title: The triangle's ceiling
beats: SOH-CAH-TOA needs a right triangle — max angle: $90°$.
But waves, orbits, and seasons turn past $90°$ constantly.
The fix: trade the triangle for a CIRCLE of radius 1.
Angles become points; points have coordinates…

steps.title: Coordinates are the values
steps.teach: Step the angle around the circle: the landing point's coordinates ARE (cos θ, sin θ) — orange leg = cosine (x), blue leg = sine (y). Cross into QII and watch cosine go negative while sine stays positive; keep going and watch every sign combination appear, quadrant by quadrant.

steps.title: Quadrant by quadrant
beats: No memorizing needed — it's just WHERE the point is:
left half → x negative; bottom half → y negative.

steps.title: Place 240°
steps.explanation: $240°$ sits in QIII (bottom-left): both coordinates negative.

examples.prompt: List $(\cos, \sin)$ at all four axis angles.
A point on the unit circle has $x = \tfrac35$ in QIV. Find its $y$.
steps: So cos $= \tfrac35$, sin $= -\tfrac45$ for this angle.

steps.title: Circle fluency
steps.intro: C is x, S is y; signs from the map.

problems.explanation: $(-1, 0)$: the y-coordinate is 0. (Half-turn = on the x-axis.)

problems.prompt: Where are BOTH cos and sin negative?
problems.explanation: Bottom-left: x and y both negative — angles between 180° and 270°.

problems.prompt: A unit-circle point has sin θ = 1. The angle is…
problems.explanation: Only the TOP of the circle has y = 1: θ = 90° (plus full turns).

steps.title: GPS runs the unit circle
steps.body: Every GPS fix solves triangles on a sphere using cos and sin at wildly obtuse angles — impossible with SOH-CAH-TOA, trivial with the circle definition. Your phone evaluates unit-circle coordinates thousands of times per fix. The 'circle upgrade' isn't a classroom nicety; it's why navigation works past the first quadrant of the planet.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Point at $\theta$ = $(\cos\theta, \sin\theta)$ — cosine x, sine y.
Axis angles read instantly; signs come from the quadrant.
$\cos^2 + \sin^2 = 1$ always: the point never leaves the circle.

lessons.title: Special Angles
lessons.concreteComparison: Three angles — $30°, 45°, 60°$ — have EXACT coordinates, inherited from geometry's two special triangles. Learn one quadrant's worth ($\tfrac12$, $\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$, $\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$) and the sign map from last lesson recycles them everywhere: $150°$, $225°$, $300°$ are the same three numbers wearing quadrant signs. Twelve values from three memories.
lessons.objective: Know the exact sine and cosine of 30°, 45°, 60° (π/6, π/4, π/3) and extend them to all quadrants via reference angles.
concept: From the special right triangles: $\cos 30° = \tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}, \sin 30° = \tfrac12$; $\cos 45° = \sin 45° = \tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$; $\cos 60° = \tfrac12, \sin 60° = \tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$.
The ladder trick: sines at $30°, 45°, 60°$ are $\tfrac{\sqrt1}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}, \tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$ — root 1, 2, 3 over 2. Cosines run the same ladder backwards.

**Reference angles**: any angle's values equal its acute reference angle's values, with the QUADRANT choosing signs. $150°$ references $30°$ (in QII): $\sin 150° = +\tfrac12$, $\cos 150° = -\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$.
Find the reference: distance to the nearest x-axis direction — $180° - 150° = 30°$; $225° - 180° = 45°$; $360° - 300° = 60°$. Three numbers + sign map = the whole circle.
lessons.keyIdea: √1/2, √2/2, √3/2 — the sine ladder at 30°, 45°, 60°. Reference angle gives the size; quadrant gives the sign.

facts.title: Reference angle
facts.explanation: Size from the reference; sign from the quadrant.

commonMistakes.correction: $\sin 30° = \tfrac12$ (small angle, small height); $\sin 60° = \tfrac{\sqrt3}{2} \approx 0.87$ (big angle, big height). Sanity-check against the picture: height grows with angle in QI.

commonMistakes.text: Getting the reference angle's SIZE right but forgetting the quadrant sign.
commonMistakes.correction: $\cos 150°$ is NOT $\tfrac{\sqrt3}{2}$ — QII is the left half, so cosine is negative. Reference gives the digits; quadrant signs them.

steps.title: Gifts from geometry
beats: hand the circle exact coordinates at $30°, 45°, 60°$:

steps.title: Root 1, 2, 3 over 2
beats: Rising angle, rising root. One pattern, six values.
Cosines climb DOWN the same ladder.

steps.title: Ladder read
steps.explanation: Cosine runs the ladder backwards: big at small angles. ($\tfrac12$ is $\sin 30°$ — the classic swap.)

steps.title: Reference angles
beats: $150°$: it leans $30°$ away from the x-axis — reference $30°$.
Same DIGITS as $30°$; QII picks the signs:
Three memories + the sign map = the whole circle.

steps: Reference $30°$, sine positive → QI and QII.

problems.explanation: The crossing point of the ladder — cos and sin agree at 45°.
Need reference $60°$ in QIII: $180 + 60 = 240°$. ($210°$ refs 30°; $300°$ is QIV.)

steps.title: Draw before you sign
steps.body: For any special-angle question: (1) sketch the circle, (2) mark the angle's quadrant, (3) find the reference by its distance to the x-axis, (4) copy the ladder digits, (5) sign by quadrant. Five seconds of sketch beats every memorized table — and survives exam nerves.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Reference angle = distance to the x-axis; it fixes the digits.
The quadrant signs them — twelve exact values from three.

lessons.title: Sine & Cosine Waves
lessons.concreteComparison: Watch one seat on a slowly turning Ferris wheel and plot its HEIGHT against time: up, over the top, down, under the bottom, forever. That plot is the **sine wave** — the unit circle's y-coordinate unrolled onto a timeline. Every wave in physics (sound, light, tides, AC power) is this one picture: circular motion, viewed sideways.
lessons.objective: Connect the unit circle to the graphs of sin and cos: shape, period 2π, amplitude 1, intercepts and peaks.
concept: Graph $y = \sin\theta$ by unrolling: as $\theta$ sweeps the circle, plot the point's HEIGHT at each angle. Starts at 0, peaks at 1 ($\theta = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$), back to 0 at $\pi$, trough $-1$ at $\tfrac{3\pi}{2}$, home at $2\pi$.
**Period** $2\pi$: one full orbit = one full wave, then it repeats forever. $\sin(\theta + 2\pi) = \sin\theta$.
**Amplitude** 1: the circle's radius caps the height — the wave lives between $-1$ and $1$.
Cosine is the X-coordinate's story: same wave, but starting at its PEAK ($\cos 0 = 1$). The cosine curve is the sine curve slid left $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$ — the transformations topic, striking again.
lessons.keyIdea: Sine = the circling point's height, unrolled: period 2π, amplitude 1. Cosine = the same wave starting at its peak.

facts.title: The wave's vitals
facts.explanation: One orbit per wave; radius caps height.

facts.title: The twins
facts.explanation: Cosine: sine, slid left a quarter-turn.

workedExamples.statement: Where in $[0, 2\pi]$ does $\sin\theta$ peak, cross zero, and trough?
workedExamples.solution: Peak 1 at $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$; zeros at $0, \pi, 2\pi$; trough $-1$ at $\tfrac{3\pi}{2}$.

workedExamples.statement: Verify the quarter-turn shift at $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \tfrac{\pi}{3}$: $\cos\theta = \sin(\theta + \tfrac{\pi}{2})$.

commonMistakes.text: Drawing the sine wave starting at its peak.
commonMistakes.correction: $\sin 0 = 0$: the sine wave starts at ZERO heading up. Starting at the peak is COSINE's signature ($\cos 0 = 1$).

commonMistakes.text: Thinking the wave eventually flattens or drifts.
commonMistakes.correction: The orbit never changes, so neither does the wave: same peaks, same period, forever. Periodicity is exact, not approximate.

tryIt.statement: $\cos\theta$ in $[0, 2\pi]$: where does it peak and trough?

steps.title: The Ferris wheel plot
beats: Track one seat's HEIGHT as the wheel turns.
Up… over the top… down… under… repeat.
Height vs time = the sine wave.
Circular motion, viewed from the side.

steps.title: The circle draws the wave
steps.teach: The orbiting point's height rides across to the scroll: as θ sweeps one full turn, the wave completes exactly one cycle — peak at π/2, zero at π, trough at 3π/2, home at 2π. One orbit = one wave = period 2π. Replay it and watch the peak happen exactly at the top of the circle.

steps.title: Read the wave
steps.explanation: The point is at the circle's BOTTOM three-quarters through the orbit.

steps.title: Cosine's head start
beats: Same shape, same period — slid left $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$:

steps.title: Which curve?
steps.prompt: A wave starts at its maximum and first hits zero at $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$. It is…
steps.explanation: Peak at the start is cosine's signature ($\cos 0 = 1$, $\cos\tfrac{\pi}{2} = 0$).

steps: Five anchor points; smooth wave through them.

examples.prompt: How many solutions does $\sin\theta = 0.3$ have in $[0, 2\pi]$?
steps: The height 0.3 is crossed once rising, once falling.
Two solutions per cycle — QI and QII.

steps.title: Unrolled fluency
steps.intro: Circle position answers every wave question.

problems.explanation: Same orbit, same period: one full turn.
The 45° crossing: both $\tfrac{\sqrt2}{2}$. (On the wave picture: where the curves intersect.)

steps.title: Every sound you hear is this graph
steps.body: A microphone diaphragm wiggles; plot its position vs time and pure tones are EXACTLY sine waves — 440 wiggles per second is concert A. Every song is a sum of sines (that's what MP3 compression exploits), every radio wave a sine at millions of cycles per second. The circle you unrolled this lesson is the alphabet of all signals.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Sine = the circling point's height, unrolled onto a timeline.
Cosine = same wave starting at its peak (slid left $\tfrac{\pi}{2}$).

lessons.title: Transforming Waves
lessons.concreteComparison: The functions unit gave you the master form $a \cdot f(x-h) + k$; waves cash it in. $y = a\sin(b\theta) + k$: the $a$ sets how TALL the wave swings (amplitude), the $b$ sets how FAST it repeats (period $= \tfrac{2\pi}{b}$), the $k$ sets the midline it swings around. Three dials, every wave in the world.
lessons.objective: Read amplitude |a|, period 2π/b, and midline k from $y = a\sin(b\theta) + k$, and evaluate such functions exactly.
concept: **Amplitude** $|a|$: half the swing. $y = 3\sin\theta$ swings $\pm3$ — the transformations unit's vertical stretch, applied to a wave.
**Period** $\tfrac{2\pi}{b}$: $y = \sin(2\theta)$ finishes a cycle when $2\theta = 2\pi$, i.e. every $\pi$ — twice as fast. Bigger $b$, faster repeat, shorter period.
**Midline** $y = k$: the vertical shift. $y = \sin\theta + 5$ swings between 4 and 6, around the line $y = 5$.
All together: $y = 3\sin(2\theta) + 5$ — amplitude 3, period $\pi$, midline 5, range $[2, 8]$. Max $= k + |a|$, min $= k - |a|$.
lessons.keyIdea: y = a·sin(bθ) + k: amplitude |a|, period 2π/b, midline k; range [k−|a|, k+|a|].

workedExamples.statement: A wave has max 10 and min 2. Find its amplitude and midline.
workedExamples.solution: Midline: average $= 6$; amplitude: half the swing $= 4$.

commonMistakes.text: Reading b itself as the period.
commonMistakes.correction: $b$ counts cycles per $2\pi$ — the period is $\tfrac{2\pi}{b}$. $y = \sin(2\theta)$ has period $\pi$ (fast), not 2.

commonMistakes.text: Taking max − min as the amplitude.
commonMistakes.correction: Max − min is the FULL swing; amplitude is HALF of it. Max 10, min 2 → amplitude 4, not 8.

steps.title: Three dials on a wave
beats: $k$: the MIDLINE (the lift dial). Old friends, new job.

steps.title: Why period = 2π/b
beats: $y = \sin(2\theta)$: the INPUT runs twice as fast.

steps.title: Count the cycles
steps.explanation: Period $\tfrac{2\pi}{4} = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$: four full waves fit in one old one.

steps.title: Dial work
steps.intro: Amplitude, period, midline — forwards and back.

examples.prompt: Full vitals of $y = 2\cos(3\theta) - 1$, and its value at $\theta = 0$.
Write a sine function with amplitude 3, period $\tfrac{2\pi}{5}$, midline 7.

steps.title: Wave dials
steps.intro: Half the swing; 2π over b; midline is home.

problems.explanation: Amplitude is $|a|$ — the flip changes direction, not tallness.

problems.prompt: A wave swings between 20 and 30. Its equation could be…

steps.title: The equalizer is a b-dial
steps.body: A music equalizer splits sound into sine components by their b values — bass sliders boost small-b (long-period) waves, treble boosts large-b ones. Audio engineers literally mix by amplitude ($a$) per frequency band ($b$). Your three dials are a recording studio's control surface.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Reverse-read: midline from the average, amplitude from half the swing.

lessons.title: Periodic Models
lessons.concreteComparison: A Ferris wheel with center 10 m up and radius 8 m turns once per minute. Your height? $h = 10 + 8\sin(\ldots)$ — the midline is the AXLE, the amplitude is the RADIUS, the period is one revolution. Anything that circles or cycles — tides, daylight hours, heartbeats — fits the same three-dial template. This lesson is the unit, applied.
lessons.objective: Build and use sinusoidal models: match midline to center, amplitude to radius/half-swing, period to the cycle time.
concept: The template: $\text{quantity} = \text{midline} + \text{amplitude} \cdot \sin(b \cdot t)$, with $b = \tfrac{2\pi}{\text{period}}$.
Ferris wheel (center 10, radius 8, period 60 s): $h = 10 + 8\sin(\tfrac{2\pi}{60}t)$ — max height $18$ (top), min $2$ (bottom), starting mid-height rising.
Reading a model: tide $d = 5 + 2\sin(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t)$ — average depth 5 m, tide swing $\pm 2$ m, period $\tfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6} = 12$ hours.
Evaluate at special times exactly: the wheel at $t = 15$ s: $\tfrac{2\pi}{60} \cdot 15 = \tfrac{\pi}{2}$, $\sin = 1$: height $18$ — the top, a quarter-turn in.
lessons.keyIdea: Midline = center; amplitude = radius (half the swing); b = 2π/period. Cycles of any kind, one template.

workedExamples.statement: Ferris wheel: center 10 m, radius 8 m, period 60 s. Write $h(t)$ and find the max/min heights.
Tide: $d = 5 + 2\sin(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t)$ ($t$ in hours). Average depth, swing, and period?
workedExamples.solution: Average 5 m; swing $\pm2$ m (3 to 7); period $\tfrac{2\pi}{\pi/6} = 12$ h.

commonMistakes.text: Setting b equal to the period.
commonMistakes.correction: $b$ is $\tfrac{2\pi}{\text{period}}$ — a 60-second wheel gets $b = \tfrac{2\pi}{60}$, not 60. Fast cycles have BIG b, long periods have small b.

commonMistakes.text: Using the wheel's TOP height as the midline.
commonMistakes.correction: The midline is the CENTER (axle) height — the average of top and bottom. Top = midline + radius; putting the top at $k$ shifts the whole ride 8 m skyward.

tryIt.statement: A wheel: center 12 m, radius 9 m. Max and min heights?

steps.title: Circle → wave → model
steps.teach: This is why the template works: the seat IS a point on a circle, and its height IS the sine wave — midline where the axle sits, amplitude the radius, period one revolution. Every periodic model in this lesson is this animation with different labels.

steps.title: Build one
steps.prompt: Wheel: center 15 m, radius 10 m, period 90 s. The model is…

steps.title: The tide chart
beats: Average depth: 5 m. High tide: 7 m. Low: 3 m.
A harbor master reads these dials for a living.

steps.title: Catch the peak
steps.prompt: The wheel $h = 10 + 8\sin(\tfrac{2\pi}{60}t)$ at $t = 45$ s is at height…

examples.prompt: Daylight in a northern city: $L = 12 + 5\sin(\tfrac{2\pi}{365}t)$ hours ($t$ = days). Longest and shortest days, and the period?
A piston's height: $y = 30 + 20\sin(4\pi t)$ cm ($t$ in seconds). Period and cycles per second?

problems.prompt: A buoy bobs between 1 m and 3 m above the seabed... its midline and amplitude are…
A wheel turns once per 30 s. Its $b$ is…

problems.explanation: Half a turn: $\sin\pi = 0$, height 10 m — halfway, now on the way down.

steps.title: The tides that run on this template
steps.body: Tide-prediction machines — brass gearboxes of cranks and pulleys built in the 1870s — summed a dozen sine waves mechanically, one per lunar/solar cycle, and printed a year of tides for any harbor. Lord Kelvin's design guided the D-Day landings. Every crank was one $a\sin(bt + \text{phase})$: this lesson, cast in brass.

steps.title: What you learned
points: Dictionary: axle → midline, radius → amplitude, revolution → period.
Evaluate at special times exactly via the unit circle.

practice.statement: Where does $\cos\theta$ trough on $[0, 2\pi]$, and what's the value?
Wheel: center 9 m, radius 7 m, period 40 s. Model and max height?

testYourself.statement: Convert $210°$ to radians and $\tfrac{5\pi}{4}$ to degrees.
A gear of radius 12 cm turns $\tfrac{5\pi}{6}$ rad. Rim distance?
Signs of cos and sin at $\theta = 305°$?

testYourself.solution: Ref $60°$, cosine negative → QII, QIII: $120°$ and $240°$.

testYourself.statement: Evaluate $y = 5\sin(2\theta) + 1$ at $\theta = \tfrac{\pi}{4}$, and give the range.
Tide: $d = 6 + 3\sin(\tfrac{\pi}{6}t)$ (hours). Depth at $t = 3$, and the low-tide depth?
